metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC4's `verify-identity --format json` proving command: verified events are omitted from the JSON report, so filter for absence of a non-verified entry for `<t>` instead — e.g., `rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>")]|length == 0'` (empty problem list for that ticket means it was verified).
evidence: verify_authorship.py:498: `report: list[dict] = []  # one entry per NON-verified in-scope event`
verify_authorship.py:503-504: `if cls == VERIFIED: continue` — verified events are never appended to `report`
verify_identity_report.schema.json description: 'The report holds one object per NON-`verified` in-scope mutating event; `verified` events are omitted.'
AC4 proving command: `rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq -e '[.[]|select(.verdict=="verified")]|any(.verdict=="verified")'`
findings.finding: AC4's `rebar verify-identity --format json` proving command filters for `select(.verdict=="verified")`, but the JSON report only contains NON-verified events (verified events are skipped with `continue` at verify_authorship.py:503-504). The filter will always return an empty array, causing `any(.verdict=="verified")` to always be false, so the command will always exit non-zero — even after signing succeeds.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4, third proving command; src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py:498-504
scenarios: After successfully provisioning the signing key and writing a signed comment, AC4's third verification step will always fail (exit non-zero) because `select(.verdict=="verified")` never matches any entry in the JSON output — making the acceptance criterion permanently unpassable as written.
findings.suggested_fix:
Replace the third proving command in AC4 with:
`rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>" and (.grandfathered|not))]|length == 0'`
This exits 0 when no non-verified, non-grandfathered events exist for the probed ticket — which is the correct signal that the new event was verified.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Correct the line-number citation in the Precondition section: `_match_by_email` is at `identity.py:119-153`; `resolve_current_identity` (which calls it) is at `identity.py:530-544`.
evidence: identity.py:119: `def _match_by_email(email: str, tracker: str) -> str | None:`
identity.py:530: `def resolve_current_identity(*, repo_root=None) -> str | None:`
Plan text: '`src/rebar/_commands/identity.py:530-544`, `_match_by_email`'
findings.finding: The plan cites `src/rebar/_commands/identity.py:530-544` as the location of `_match_by_email`, but those lines actually contain `resolve_current_identity`. The `_match_by_email` function is at lines 119-153. While the behavioral description is accurate, the mislabeled citation could mislead someone auditing the code path.
scenarios: A reviewer or executor auditing the cited line range finds resolve_current_identity, not _match_by_email, and may doubt the plan's accuracy or spend time tracing the call.
findings.suggested_fix: Change '`src/rebar/_commands/identity.py:530-544`, `_match_by_email`' to '`src/rebar/_commands/identity.py:530-544` (`resolve_current_identity`, which calls `_match_by_email` at lines 119-153)'.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC4's `verify-identity --format json` proving command: since verified events are excluded from the JSON report, replace the jq filter with one that checks for ABSENCE of the ticket in the non-verified report (e.g., `jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>")]|length==0'`), or use the text-mode summary line (`rebar verify-identity --all | grep 'verified'`), or check the `authorship.signed` count that is already proven by the preceding `rebar show` step.
evidence: src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py:498: `report: list[dict] = []  # one entry per NON-verified in-scope event`
src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py:503-504: `if cls == VERIFIED: continue` — verified events are never appended to `report`
src/rebar/_commands/verify_authorship.py:531-533: JSON mode prints `json.dumps(report)` — the array that omits verified events
findings.finding: AC4's `verify-identity --format json` proving command filters for `verdict=="verified"` entries, but the implementation only emits NON-verified events to the JSON report array — verified events are explicitly skipped (`continue` at verify_authorship.py:503-504). The jq filter `any(.verdict=="verified")` will always return false/fail for a correctly-signed event, making the AC permanently unprovable as written.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (fresh-worktree signing probe), `rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>")]|any(.verdict=="verified")'`
scenarios: An executor runs the AC4 proving command after successfully signing the probe comment. The event IS verified, but the jq filter finds no matching entry with verdict=verified in the JSON (because verified events are excluded from the output). The command exits non-zero, making AC4 appear to fail even when signing works correctly.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the final verify-identity jq command in AC4 with: `rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>" and .verdict!="verified")]|length==0'` — this passes when no non-verified events exist for the ticket, which is the correct success condition given the implementation.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC4's `verify-identity --format json` proving command: since verified events are omitted from the report, invert the filter to confirm the newly-written event is ABSENT from the non-verified list (e.g., `jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>")]|length==0'`), or use a different command such as `rebar show <t>` checking `authorship.signed >= 1`.
evidence: Plan AC4: `rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq -e '[.[]|select(.verdict=="verified")]|any(.verdict=="verified")'`
Schema description in `src/rebar/schemas/verify_identity_report.schema.json:5`: 'The report holds one object per NON-`verified` in-scope mutating event; `verified` events are omitted.'

`display` field enum `["verified","unverified","unsigned"]` at `verify_identity_report.schema.json:15` — these are display labels for non-verified entries, not markers of genuinely verified events appearing in the output
ABSENCE: no mechanism in the schema or implementation emits verified events into the JSON array, so `select(.verdict=="verified")` can never match
findings.finding: AC4's `rebar verify-identity --format json` proving command filters for `select(.verdict=="verified")`, but the JSON report schema (`verify_identity_report.schema.json`) explicitly states it 'holds one object per NON-`verified` in-scope mutating event; `verified` events are omitted.' The `display` field has enum `["verified","unverified","unsigned"]` but these classify non-verified entries only. A jq filter for `verdict=="verified"` will always return an empty array (never `any(...)` = true), making this AC unprovable by construction even after successful signing setup.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 (fresh worktree signing proof), third proving command
scenarios: An operator runs AC4 after correctly provisioning the signing key; the jq command exits non-zero (no matches) even though signing is working correctly, causing a false AC failure
An operator concludes the signing setup is broken and re-attempts provisioning, wasting time chasing a phantom failure
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the third AC4 proving command with one that matches the report's actual semantics — verified events are absent from the report, so the correct proof is absence: `rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>")]|length==0'` (the ticket has zero non-verified events after signing). Alternatively, rely solely on the second proving command (`rebar show <t> | jq -e '.authorship.signed >= 1'`) which directly checks the signed-event counter.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Fix AC4's `rebar verify-identity` proving command: verified events are excluded from the JSON report, so the correct check is ABSENCE of the ticket's events in the report (e.g. `jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>")]|length == 0'`) rather than presence of a `.verdict=="verified"` entry.
evidence: `verify_authorship.py:503-504`: `if cls == VERIFIED: continue` — verified events are not appended to `report`
`verify_identity_report.schema.json` description: 'The report holds one object per NON-`verified` in-scope mutating event; `verified` events are omitted.'
`verify_authorship.py:498`: `report: list[dict] = []  # one entry per NON-verified in-scope event`
Evidence class: Contradicted — the plan asserts `.verdict=="verified"` but the implementation proves verified events produce no report entry.
findings.finding: AC4's proving command `rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>")]|any(.verdict=="verified")'` will always fail (return false) for a correctly signed event, because `verify_authorship.py:503-504` explicitly skips verified events from the JSON report array (`if cls == VERIFIED: continue`). The JSON output contains ONLY non-verified events. A correctly provisioned signing setup produces zero entries for the probed ticket in the report — not an entry with `.verdict=="verified"`.
findings.location: AC4 proving command: `rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>")]|any(.verdict=="verified")'`
scenarios: An operator runs AC4 after successful provisioning; the jq expression returns false because the newly signed event is absent from the JSON array (it was verified), causing the acceptance criterion to falsely fail.

Alternatively: if the signing setup is broken and the event IS in the report, it will carry a non-verified verdict, and the jq expression still returns false — the command cannot distinguish success from failure.
findings.suggested_fix: Replace the final proving command in AC4 with: `rebar verify-identity --all --format json | jq -e '[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>")]|length == 0'` (exits 0 when the target ticket has no non-verified events in scope, i.e. all its events are verified).

evidence: verify command `ls -l ~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key` inspects output with `ls` but asserts nothing — add an exit-code or comparison (-q, [ "$(...)" -eq N ], jq -e) so a failure is observable
findings.suggested_fix: Split compound criteria, replace subjective terms with observable outcomes, and state how each is verified.

evidence: file_impact is empty
findings.finding: No file_impact declared on a leaf work ticket.
findings.suggested_fix: Record the {path, reason} files this work will touch (e.g. via set_file_impact) so the attestation is scoped to them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Either add a minimal CLAUDE.md note (or ADR stub) capturing the 'use `~/.config/rebar/config.toml` for per-machine signing key, not env-var or project rebar.toml' decision in this story, or explicitly confirm as an accepted risk that 8d39 will land before any second machine needs provisioning.
evidence: Plan quote: 'A shared CLAUDE.md/ADR record of the pattern is deferred to the enforcement-flip story (8d39).'
The rationale for user-layer-over-env-var is captured only inline in this plan text, not in any committed doc artifact.
AC5 captures a local runbook, but that is a per-machine operator record, not a shared architectural record.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel architectural decision — using the XDG user-layer config (`~/.config/rebar/config.toml`) as the canonical, worktree-independent per-machine signing-key provisioning mechanism (preferred over env-var export) — but explicitly defers the shared CLAUDE.md/ADR documentation of this pattern to story 8d39. Until 8d39 lands, any operator setting up a second machine (or the next bot clone) has no shared design record explaining the choice or the correct layer/file to use.
findings.location: Scope section: 'A shared CLAUDE.md/ADR record of the pattern is deferred to the enforcement-flip story (8d39).'
scenarios: A second developer or CI operator needs to provision signing on another machine before 8d39 is completed and has no ADR/CLAUDE.md to follow — they may resort to the env-var approach or a project-level rebar.toml entry, both of which this plan explicitly rules out.
Story 8d39 is deprioritized or dropped; the pattern stays undocumented indefinitely.
findings.suggested_fix: Add a single CLAUDE.md entry (3–5 lines) under a 'Per-machine signing key' heading: state the canonical file path, the config key, and why the user layer is preferred over REBAR_IDENTITY_SIGNING_KEY. Mark it 'full ADR in 8d39'. This keeps the story's 'no tracked repo files' scope claim — CLAUDE.md is a tracked file, so adjust the scope note if you add it, or accept the deferral as a documented risk.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Document the expected AWS CLI invocation and the error-handling steps (credential check, parameter name verification, IAM permission required) so the operator gets an actionable error, not a silent or opaque failure.
evidence: 'Materialize the Rebar Bot private key from AWS SSM SecureString /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key (ADR-0012)'
No mention of aws CLI error codes, credential checks, or fallback if the parameter is absent or the operator lacks IAM permission.
findings.finding: The plan names AWS SSM (`/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key`) as the source for the private key but specifies no error handling for SSM unavailability (network failure, expired credentials, missing parameter, wrong region). No timeout, fallback, or operator-visible error surfacing is described.
findings.location: What section — 'Materialize the Rebar Bot private key from AWS SSM SecureString /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key'
scenarios: Operator runs provisioning on a new machine with stale/absent AWS credentials — SSM call fails silently or with an opaque AWS error, no guidance on what to do next.
Parameter is renamed or moved in SSM — provisioning fails with no diagnostic pointing at the root cause.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the runbook: `aws ssm get-parameter --name /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key --with-decryption --query Parameter.Value --output text > ~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key && chmod 0600 ~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key` with a preceding `aws sts get-caller-identity` to verify credentials, and explicit notes on required IAM action `ssm:GetParameter`.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the AWS credential mechanism the local operator must use to read `/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` from SSM (e.g., AWS SSO session via `aws sso login`, a scoped IAM role assumed with short-lived credentials, or the specific minimum IAM actions required), and add this to AC5 / the local runbook.
evidence: Plan states 'Materialize the Rebar Bot private key from AWS SSM SecureString /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key' with no specification of the local AWS credential mechanism.
infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh authenticates via EC2 instance role (IMDSv2) — unavailable on a local workstation.
AC5 references 'local runbook' but the runbook content is deferred, so no credential guidance is captured in the plan itself.
ABSENCE: no mention of AWS SSO, role assumption, or credential type for the local operator performing the SSM fetch.
findings.finding: The plan instructs the operator to fetch the private key from SSM (/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key) on a local workstation but never specifies what AWS IAM identity or credential mechanism the operator uses. The EC2 instance role (used by fetch-secrets.sh on the box) is unavailable locally. Using long-lived IAM user access keys for this operation would violate least-privilege; using AWS SSO or a short-lived role assumption would be correct — but the plan is silent on which mechanism is required.
findings.location: What section — 'Materialize the Rebar Bot private key from AWS SSM SecureString /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key'
scenarios: Operator provisions long-lived IAM user access keys in ~/.aws/credentials to satisfy this step, violating least-privilege.
Another operator trying to reprovision the key on a different workstation has no guidance on what IAM permissions are needed, leading to trial-and-error or over-privileged credential use.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section or AC5, add: 'The operator reads the SSM parameter using a short-lived credential — e.g., `aws sso login --profile <rebar-ops-profile>` then `aws ssm get-parameter --name /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key --with-decryption --query Parameter.Value --output text --profile <rebar-ops-profile>`. Long-lived IAM user keys must not be used for this operation.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Declare the minimum IAM permission required for the local SSM fetch in the What section or AC5 runbook (e.g., `ssm:GetParameter` scoped to `arn:aws:ssm:<region>:<account>:parameter/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` only), and note what credential/role the operator should use — do not leave the IAM access posture undeclared for this boundary-crossing step.
evidence: Plan states: 'Materialize the Rebar Bot private key from AWS SSM SecureString /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key (ADR-0012)' but specifies no IAM identity or permission scope for the local operator.

infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh lines 46-58: the SSM namespace /rebar/prod/ also contains anthropic-api-key, mcp-hmac-signing-key, gerrit-admin-password, gerrit-bot-token — broad read access to this namespace exposes all of them.
ABSENCE: no AC step verifies or declares the operator's IAM permission scope (e.g., 'operator must have ssm:GetParameter on the single param ARN only').
T5c least-privilege check: 'any credential/role/grant a boundary-crossing component holds is scoped to the minimum; no wildcard / admin-for-convenience'
findings.finding: The SSM fetch step (`aws ssm get-parameter --with-decryption /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key`) crosses the local-workstation→AWS-SSM trust boundary, but the plan declares no IAM access posture for the local operator performing the fetch. The `/rebar/prod/` namespace also holds other high-value production secrets (anthropic-api-key, gerrit-admin-password, gerrit-bot-token per infra/scripts/fetch-secrets.sh). Without a declared minimum IAM grant, an operator may use a broad credential (e.g., ssm:GetParameter on `arn:aws:ssm:*:*:parameter/rebar/prod/*` or administrator), violating least-privilege at the only boundary-crossing step in this plan.
findings.location: What section — 'Materialize the Rebar Bot private key from AWS SSM SecureString /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key'
scenarios: Operator runs `aws ssm get-parameter` with a broad IAM role (e.g., PowerUser or ssm:GetParameter on /rebar/prod/*), silently gaining read access to all production secrets in the namespace — this is never flagged because the plan sets no constraint.
A future audit of IAM grants has no declared-minimum to compare against, making least-privilege drift undetectable.
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC5 (or the What section): 'The operator performing the SSM fetch must hold an IAM grant of `ssm:GetParameter` + `kms:Decrypt` scoped to the single parameter ARN `arn:aws:ssm:<region>:<account>:parameter/rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key` — not a wildcard grant on `/rebar/prod/*`. Verify with `aws iam simulate-principal-policy` or confirm via the local runbook that the active credential is the narrowly-scoped bot-key-reader role.'

findings.finding: The plan's AC3 asserts 'The identity's registered public key matches the configured private key, and `git config user.email` matches the identity email' — this presupposes that an identity ticket for `joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com` already exists in the store with a registered public key. The plan never states that creating this identity ticket is part of the work (or that it already exists), yet `resolve_current_identity` can only resolve the email if such a ticket is present. No identity ticket for this email is verifiable from repo files.
findings.location: AC3 / What section — 'Confirm the writer's git config user.email (joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com) resolves to an identity whose registered public key matches the configured private key'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the What section: 'Verify that an identity ticket for joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com exists (run `rebar show <id>` or scan with `rebar identity use`); if absent, create it with `rebar identity create --name "..." --email joeoakhart+bot@navapbc.com --key "<pub key line>" --self`'.

findings.finding: The plan states 'Only ~4 one-off validation-demo signed events exist store-wide', but the committed `rebar.toml` comment (the rollout-prep note) explicitly says 'The store currently holds 0 signed events'. These contradict each other. The plan's ~4-event premise is unverified and conflicts with a codebase artifact.
findings.location: Why section — 'Only ~4 one-off validation-demo signed events exist store-wide'; rebar.toml line 85
findings.suggested_fix: Run `rebar fsck` (or `rebar verify-authorship --all`) to get the actual signed-event count, then update the `rebar.toml` comment OR the plan's Why section to match reality.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace `<t>` in AC4's proving commands with a concrete ticket selection rule (e.g., 'pick any existing open ticket; record its id in the attestation comment') or a named ticket id, so the commands are copy-paste executable.
evidence: 'rebar comment <t> "signing-probe"'
'rebar show <t> | jq -e \'.authorship.signed >= 1\''
'jq -e \'[.[]|select(.ticket_id=="<t>")]|any(.verdict=="verified")\''
No sentence in the plan names a concrete ticket id or selection rule for <t>
findings.finding: AC4's proving commands use `<t>` as a literal placeholder in three places but never specify how the executor should select which ticket to use as the signing probe target. An executor following the plan verbatim cannot run the commands without an unstated decision.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC4 ('A fresh worktree signs with no per-session setup')
scenarios: An executing agent runs the proving command literally with `<t>` un-substituted and gets a shell or jq error, blocking AC4 verification.
Two executors pick different tickets and get inconsistent results if one ticket has no prior events or is in an unexpected state.
findings.suggested_fix: State a default in the plan: 'Use the ticket id of this story itself as `<t>` (594c-9dcf-5ad6-4e6d or any accessible ticket); substitute the actual id before running.' Alternatively, add a setup line: 'Set T=<any-accessible-ticket-id> and substitute below.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify how to safely add/update only the `[identity] signing_key` stanza in a pre-existing `~/.config/rebar/config.toml` (e.g., use `rebar config set` or a TOML-aware merge), and document the key-rotation procedure (update key file → re-run AC2 diff check → confirm signing still works).
evidence: 'Configure [identity] signing_key = "~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key" in the XDG user config ~/.config/rebar/config.toml — the per-machine, git-ignored config the loader merges'
No mention of pre-existing file contents, safe merge/append strategy, or what to do when the private key is rotated or compromised.
'No RETIRE/cleanup path' for the config entry or the key file.
findings.finding: The plan writes `[identity] signing_key = ...` into `~/.config/rebar/config.toml` but does not specify whether this file pre-exists, what its current contents are, or how to safely add only the signing_key stanza without destroying other user-level config that may already be present. No RETIRE/rotation path is specified for when the key must be replaced.
findings.location: What section — 'Configure [identity] signing_key = "~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key" in the XDG user config ~/.config/rebar/config.toml'
scenarios: ~/.config/rebar/config.toml already contains other rebar user settings; a naive overwrite destroys them.
The SSM key is rotated for security reasons — the plan gives no guidance on updating the key file and re-verifying AC2 without a full re-provisioning.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (or extend AC4's proving command) that asserts `rebar.toml` contains no `signing_key` entry at the project layer, or restate the What-section guarantee as conditional ('wins because rebar.toml sets no signing_key') and note the dependency explicitly.
evidence: "LAYER_ORDER = default,user,project,env,cli; the project rebar.toml sets no signing_key, so the user value wins in every worktree"
ABSENCE: no AC checks `rebar.toml` (or any project-layer config) for absence of a `signing_key` entry
Project layer comes after user in LAYER_ORDER, so any future project-level signing_key would silently shadow the machine config with no warning
findings.finding: The What section claims the user-layer config 'wins in **every** worktree' as if this is a structural property of the config mechanism, but the LAYER_ORDER it cites (`default,user,project,env,cli`) places `project` after `user`, meaning a project-level `signing_key` in `rebar.toml` would silently override it. The guarantee is only conditionally true because `rebar.toml` currently has no `signing_key` — but no AC verifies that precondition, so the cross-section assertion (What: absolute guarantee; AC set: tests only the outcome) is unsupported.
findings.location: What section — '…the user value wins in **every** worktree'; AC4
scenarios: A contributor adds `signing_key` to `rebar.toml` (e.g. for CI), instantly overriding the machine config and causing unsigned local writes with no diagnostic
Implementer reads the absolute claim and does not verify the rebar.toml precondition, shipping a setup that is fragile to project-config changes
findings.suggested_fix: Amend the What section to read: 'the user value wins in every worktree **as long as the project `rebar.toml` defines no `signing_key`** (currently true; must not be set there).' Add a lightweight AC: `grep -v 'signing_key' rebar.toml` exits 0, or equivalently add a note to the runbook that rebar.toml must never carry a signing_key.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the safe write ordering (key file first, then config) and add an idempotency check (e.g., verify key file exists and is valid before writing config) so partial provisioning is detectable and re-runnable without corruption.
evidence: 'Materialize the Rebar Bot private key from AWS SSM SecureString /rebar/prod/rebar-bot-signing-key ... to a 0600 file ... at ~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key'
'Configure [identity] signing_key = "~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key" in the XDG user config ~/.config/rebar/config.toml'
No ordering, atomicity, or partial-state recovery mentioned between the two writes.
findings.finding: The provisioning sequence (SSM fetch → write key file → write config file) has no specified error handling or ordering. If the SSM fetch succeeds but the key write fails, or the key is written but the config write fails, the machine is left in a partial state where rebar.toml points to a key that doesn't exist (or no config yet points to an existing key). The plan specifies no recovery or idempotency check for this two-step write.
findings.location: What section — 'Materialize' and 'Configure' bullets; AC5 operator-attested step
scenarios: SSM fetch returns the key, operator is interrupted before config is written — next session has no config pointing at the key, no signing occurs, silently.
Config is written before key is materialized — every rebar write immediately attempts signing and fails with a missing-key error.
findings.suggested_fix: In the runbook captured by AC5, document the explicit ordering: (1) fetch and write key file, (2) verify `ssh-keygen -y -f ~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key` succeeds, (3) only then write the config stanza. Make each step re-entrant: if the key file already exists and its public half matches, skip step 1.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve tilde expansion: either (a) prescribe an absolute path for `signing_key` in config.toml (e.g. `/home/<user>/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key`) so no expansion is needed, or (b) add `Path(signing_key).expanduser()` before passing to `sign_event_authorship` in `_seam.py`, and update the plan's AC2 proving command accordingly.
evidence: Plan specifies: `[identity] signing_key = "~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key"`
_seam.py:285: `signing_key = cfg.identity.signing_key` (raw str from TOML)
_seam.py:298-299: `authorship.sign_event_authorship(event, signing_key, ...)` — key passed as-is
authorship.py:54: `sig = sshsig.sign(pae, key_path, ...)` — no expanduser
sshsig.py:80-81: `subprocess.run(["ssh-keygen", "-Y", "sign", "-f", key_path, ...])` — argv, no shell, no tilde expansion
config.py:335: the only expanduser call is for finding the config file itself, not key_path values within it
_seam.py:306-308: BLE001 handler catches any signing failure and writes unsigned — silent failure, no loud error
findings.finding: The plan configures `signing_key = "~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key"` in the user config TOML, but the codebase passes `cfg.identity.signing_key` directly as an argv element to `subprocess.run(["ssh-keygen", "-Y", "sign", "-f", key_path, ...])` with no `Path.expanduser()` call anywhere in the chain (`_seam.py:285,299` → `authorship.py:54` → `sshsig.py:81`). Shell tilde expansion is not applied to subprocess argv — ssh-keygen will receive `~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key` as a literal path, fail silently (the BLE001 handler catches the exception and writes unsigned), and AC4 will not pass.
scenarios: Implementer writes `signing_key = "~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key"` in config.toml; ssh-keygen receives `~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key` as a literal argv string; key not found; signing silently falls back to unsigned; AC4 (`rebar verify-identity --all` shows `verified`) fails with no clear error message.
AC5 attestation (operator records mode line) would still pass since the key file itself can be created correctly; only the signing integration would be broken.
findings.suggested_fix: In the What section, replace `signing_key = "~/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key"` with the absolute expanded path, e.g. `signing_key = "/home/joeoakhart/.ssh/rebar-bot-signing-key"` (or `"$HOME/..."` if the config loader supports env expansion — verify first). Alternatively, patch `_seam.py:285` to apply `Path(signing_key).expanduser()` and update the What section to note the patch.

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
